text: The patient is positioned supine in reverse Trendelenburg with the left side elevated at a fifteen-degree angle. After diagnostic laparoscopy confirms no metastatic disease, the robot is docked. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the gastrocolic ligament inferior to the gastroepiploic vessels. The body of the pancreas becomes visible through the surgical window. A large splenic artery, approximately twelve millimeters in diameter, is seen pulsating with a serpentine course. Dissection along the greater curvature continues to the angle of His, completing mobilization.

With the stomach reflected cephalad, the surgeon incises the peritoneum along the inferior margin of the pancreas and performs a medial to lateral dissection. The omentum and splenocolic ligament are divided, fully mobilizing the splenic flexure of the colon. Using hook cautery, the peritoneum at the superior margin of the pancreas is divided, exposing the splenic artery. The surgeon then elevates the pancreas from the retroperitoneum to expose the splenic vein.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to perform fine dissection, separating the splenic vein from the pancreas. The dissection is carried underneath the splenic vein and progresses towards the splenic artery, with the splenic vein now encircled.

The surgeon retracts the splenic artery cephalad, revealing the dorsal pancreatic artery branching off inferiorly. A vessel loop is brought through the passageway under the neck of the pancreas for preparation of division. The pancreas, noted to be thick, is divided using energy after attempts with a stapler are abandoned. The pancreatic duct is identified, suture ligated with a 3-0 silk suture, and divided with a harmonic scalpel, exposing the splenic vessels. The artery, larger than the vein, is divided first using a tan load of staples, followed by the vein. The retroperitoneal dissection begins, elevating the pancreas, with adhesions to the transverse mesocolon noted and partially taken with the specimen.

The surgeon begins to dissect past the pancreatic tumor, taking down the peritoneal reflection to facilitate further exposure and access. The robotic arms are docked from the head of the patient, providing a clear view and control for the upper abdominal dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection by taking down the remaining peritoneal reflection supporting the spleen, freeing the specimen. The pancreatic bed is irrigated and inspected for hemostasis. Three polypropylene mattress sutures are placed across the pancreas, and a drain is left along the pancreatic bed. The specimen is placed in a large container for retrieval via the supraumbilical site.
